definition: The estimated market value of a property after all renovations and repairs are completed. ARV is the foundation of every investment deal analysis — it tells you what the property will be worth when you sell or refinance. To calculate ARV, pull comparable sales (comps) of similar, recently renovated properties within a half-mile radius.

A legal transfer of a real estate purchase contract from the original buyer (wholesaler) to a new buyer (end investor). The wholesaler earns an assignment fee for connecting the seller with the buyer without ever taking ownership of the property. Assignment contracts must include an assignment clause in the original purchase agreement.

The profit a wholesaler earns when assigning a purchase contract to an end buyer. The fee is the difference between the wholesaler's contract price with the seller and the price the end buyer pays. Typical assignment fees range from $5,000 to $50,000 per deal depending on the property value and spread.

A real estate investing strategy that stands for Buy, Rehab, Rent, Refinance, Repeat. The investor buys a distressed property below market value, renovates it, rents it out, then does a cash-out refinance to recover the initial investment. The recovered capital is used to repeat the process on a new property, building a rental portfolio with recycled capital.

Capitalization rate — the ratio of a property's net operating income (NOI) to its purchase price, expressed as a percentage. Cap rate measures the expected annual return on an investment property independent of financing. Formula: Cap Rate = (NOI / Purchase Price) × 100. A higher cap rate indicates higher returns but typically higher risk. Most investors target 5-10% cap rates depending on the market.

The net income remaining after all expenses are paid on a rental property each month. Cash flow = Monthly Rent - Mortgage Payment - Property Taxes - Insurance - Maintenance - Vacancy Reserve - Property Management. Positive cash flow means the property generates profit each month. Most investors target $100-$300+ per unit per month in cash flow.

The annual cash flow divided by the total cash invested, expressed as a percentage. Unlike cap rate, cash-on-cash return accounts for financing — it measures the actual return on the money you put in, not the total property value. Formula: CoC = (Annual Cash Flow / Total Cash Invested) × 100. Most investors target 8-12% or higher.

Recent sales of similar properties in the same area used to estimate a property's market value. Good comps share similar characteristics: same neighborhood (within 0.5 miles), similar size (within 20%), similar age and condition, sold within the last 6 months. Comps are the foundation of property valuation for both investors and appraisers.

A report that compares a subject property to recently sold similar properties to estimate its market value. CMAs are prepared by real estate agents and investors using MLS data, public records, and comparable sales. A thorough CMA includes at least 3-5 comparable properties with adjustments for differences in size, condition, location, and features.

The ratio of a property's net operating income to its annual debt payments. DSCR = NOI / Annual Debt Service. A DSCR above 1.0 means the property generates enough income to cover its loan payments. Most lenders require a DSCR of 1.2-1.25 or higher for investment property loans. DSCR loans allow investors to qualify based on property income rather than personal income.

A wholesale exit strategy where two separate closings happen back-to-back — the wholesaler first buys the property from the seller (A-to-B closing), then immediately sells it to the end buyer (B-to-C closing). Unlike assignment, the wholesaler briefly takes title to the property. Double closings hide the wholesaler's profit from both parties and are useful when the assignment fee is large.

The investigation and research period after a property goes under contract but before closing. Due diligence includes property inspection, title search, reviewing liens and encumbrances, verifying property boundaries, checking zoning compliance, estimating repair costs, and confirming the property's value. Most purchase contracts include a due diligence period (typically 7-30 days) during which the buyer can back out.

A good-faith deposit made by the buyer to the seller when a purchase contract is executed, showing the buyer is serious about the transaction. EMD is typically held in escrow by a title company or attorney. For wholesale deals, EMD is usually $500-$5,000. For retail purchases, it is typically 1-3% of the purchase price. The deposit is credited toward the purchase at closing or refunded if the buyer backs out during the due diligence period.

The difference between a property's current market value and the amount owed on the mortgage. Equity = Market Value - Mortgage Balance. Equity builds through mortgage paydown (the tenant's rent pays off your loan), appreciation (property values increase over time), and forced appreciation (renovations that increase value). Investors can access equity through cash-out refinancing or selling.

A neutral third party (typically a title company or escrow agent) that holds funds and documents during a real estate transaction. Escrow ensures that the seller delivers clear title and the buyer delivers the purchase funds simultaneously at closing. The escrow agent also holds earnest money deposits during the contract period.

A real estate investment strategy where an investor buys a distressed property below market value, renovates it, and sells it for profit. The profit comes from the spread between the all-in cost (purchase price + rehab + holding costs + selling costs) and the sale price. Successful flippers buy at 65-75% of ARV minus repairs and target a minimum profit of $25,000-$50,000 per deal.

A quick metric for evaluating rental properties. GRM = Property Price / Annual Gross Rent. A lower GRM indicates a better deal. For example, a $200,000 property renting for $2,000/month has a GRM of 8.3 ($200,000 / $24,000). Most investors look for GRMs under 10 for cash-flowing properties. GRM is a rough screening tool — always follow up with a full cash flow analysis.

A short-term, asset-based loan provided by private lenders (not banks) for real estate investments. Hard money loans are based on the property's value (typically 65-75% LTV) rather than the borrower's creditworthiness. Terms: 6-18 month duration, 10-14% interest, 2-4 origination points. Hard money is ideal for fix-and-flip projects and bridge financing where speed matters more than cost.

An investment strategy where you buy a small multifamily property (duplex, triplex, or fourplex), live in one unit, and rent out the others. The rental income from the other units covers most or all of your mortgage payment, effectively letting you live for free while building equity. House hacking qualifies for owner-occupied financing (FHA loans with 3.5% down), making it one of the lowest capital entry points into real estate investing.

The ratio of a loan amount to the appraised value of the property, expressed as a percentage. LTV = Loan Amount / Property Value × 100. A $150,000 loan on a $200,000 property has a 75% LTV. Lower LTV means less risk for the lender and typically better loan terms for the borrower. Most conventional loans require 75-80% LTV (20-25% down payment) for investment properties.

The highest price an investor should pay for a property based on the deal analysis. The standard formula for wholesale/flip deals: MAO = ARV × 70% - Repair Costs - Assignment Fee (if wholesaling). This ensures there is enough profit margin for the end buyer while leaving room for the wholesaler's fee. Some investors use 65% or 75% depending on market conditions and risk tolerance.

A property owner who has a strong reason to sell quickly, often willing to accept a below-market price in exchange for speed and certainty. Common motivations include pre-foreclosure, inherited property, divorce, job relocation, tax delinquency, costly repairs, tired landlord syndrome, and code violations. Finding motivated sellers is the core skill in wholesaling and discounted acquisitions.

The annual income a property generates after all operating expenses but before debt service (mortgage payments). NOI = Gross Rental Income - Operating Expenses. Operating expenses include property taxes, insurance, maintenance, property management, vacancy allowance, and utilities (if owner-paid). NOI does NOT include mortgage payments, depreciation, or capital expenditures. NOI is the basis for calculating cap rate.

An individual (not an institutional lender) who loans their own money for real estate investments. Private money lenders are typically friends, family, colleagues, or members of real estate investment groups. Terms are negotiable and usually more flexible than hard money — interest rates of 6-12%, longer terms, and less rigid qualification requirements. Building relationships with private lenders is a key scaling strategy for investors.

The total cost of renovating an investment property, including materials, labor, permits, and contractor fees. Accurate rehab estimation is critical — underestimating rehab costs is the #1 reason flips lose money. Common rehab categories: kitchen ($15-30K), bathrooms ($8-15K each), flooring ($3-8K), paint ($3-5K), roof ($5-15K), HVAC ($4-10K), foundation ($5-20K). Always add a 10-20% contingency buffer.

The percentage return on the total money invested in a real estate deal. ROI = (Net Profit / Total Investment) × 100. For flips: Net Profit = Sale Price - Purchase Price - Rehab - Holding Costs - Selling Costs. For rentals: use annual cash flow or total return (cash flow + appreciation + equity paydown + tax benefits). ROI should be compared to alternative investments to determine if the deal is worth pursuing.

A detailed document listing every repair, renovation, and improvement to be made on an investment property. The SOW includes specific tasks (demo, framing, electrical, plumbing, etc.), materials specifications, labor estimates, and a timeline. A clear SOW is essential for getting accurate contractor bids, managing the renovation budget, and avoiding scope creep that inflates costs.

The process of finding contact information (phone number, email, mailing address) for a property owner using public records, databases, and data aggregation services. Skip tracing is essential for off-market deal finding — once you identify a potentially motivated seller's property, you need their contact info to make an offer. Popular skip tracing services include BatchSkipTracing, TLOxp, and REISkip.

A creative financing strategy where the buyer takes ownership of a property 'subject to' the existing mortgage remaining in the seller's name. The buyer makes the mortgage payments but the loan stays in the seller's name. This allows investors to acquire properties without qualifying for a new loan. Risks include the due-on-sale clause (lender could call the loan due) and the seller remaining liable for the mortgage.

A tax-deferral strategy under IRS Section 1031 that allows investors to sell an investment property and reinvest the proceeds into a 'like-kind' property without paying capital gains taxes on the sale. Requirements: the replacement property must be identified within 45 days and closed within 180 days. A qualified intermediary must hold the funds. 1031 exchanges allow investors to scale their portfolio without losing capital to taxes.

A quick formula used by wholesalers and flippers to determine the maximum purchase price for an investment property. The rule states: Maximum Offer = ARV × 70% - Repair Costs. The 30% margin covers closing costs, holding costs, selling costs, and profit. Example: a property with an ARV of $200,000 and $30,000 in repairs should be purchased for no more than $110,000 ($200,000 × 0.70 - $30,000).

The rate at which available homes are sold in a specific market during a given time period. Absorption Rate = Number of Homes Sold / Total Available Inventory. A high absorption rate (above 20%) indicates a seller's market with high demand. A low rate (below 15%) indicates a buyer's market with excess supply. Investors use absorption rate to gauge market health and determine pricing strategy.

The increase in a property's value over time. There are two types: natural appreciation (driven by market forces like population growth and inflation, typically 3-5% annually) and forced appreciation (created by the investor through renovations, better management, or rezoning). Appreciation is one of the four wealth-building pillars of real estate alongside cash flow, principal paydown, and tax benefits.

A professional assessment of a property's market value conducted by a licensed appraiser. Lenders require appraisals before issuing mortgages to ensure the property is worth at least the loan amount. Appraisers use comparable sales, property condition, location, and market trends to determine value. For investors, the appraisal is critical for refinancing — it determines how much equity you can access.

Small directional signs (typically 18×24 inches) placed at intersections and high-traffic areas with messages like 'We Buy Houses' or 'Cash for Your Home.' Bandit signs are a low-cost marketing method used by wholesalers and investors to generate motivated seller leads. They cost $1-5 each and can generate 5-15 calls per sign per week. Note: many cities have ordinances against bandit signs, so check local laws before placing them.

A person who finds potential real estate deals and passes them to investors in exchange for a referral fee (typically $500-$2,000 per deal). Bird dogging is an entry-level role in real estate investing — it requires no capital or contracts, just the ability to identify distressed or undervalued properties. Bird dogs are essentially lead generators for wholesalers and investors.

Tax owed on the profit from selling an investment property. Short-term capital gains (properties held less than 1 year) are taxed as ordinary income (up to 37%). Long-term capital gains (properties held more than 1 year) are taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income. Investors can defer capital gains using 1031 exchanges or offset them with depreciation.

An investor who purchases properties with cash rather than financing. Cash buyers can close faster (7-14 days vs 30-45 days with financing), have no appraisal contingencies, and can often negotiate better prices due to the certainty of closing. Wholesalers sell primarily to cash buyers. Building a cash buyers list is essential for successful wholesaling.

A refinancing strategy where you replace your existing mortgage with a new, larger loan and pocket the difference in cash. For example, if your property is worth $200,000 and you owe $120,000, you could refinance at 75% LTV ($150,000) and receive $30,000 in cash. This is the 'Refinance' step in the BRRRR method — it allows you to recover your initial investment and redeploy it into the next deal.

Fees and expenses paid at the closing of a real estate transaction beyond the purchase price. Buyer closing costs typically include title insurance, escrow fees, recording fees, loan origination fees, and prepaid taxes/insurance (usually 2-5% of purchase price). Seller closing costs include real estate agent commissions (5-6%), transfer taxes, title fees, and outstanding liens. Investors must factor closing costs into their deal analysis.

Non-traditional methods of funding real estate deals that go beyond conventional bank loans. Creative financing strategies include seller financing, subject-to, lease options, private money, hard money, partnerships, self-directed IRAs, and wraparound mortgages. These strategies allow investors to acquire properties with less money down, no bank qualification, or more favorable terms than traditional financing.

The number of days a property has been listed for sale on the MLS before going under contract. DOM is a key market health indicator — low DOM (under 30 days) indicates a hot seller's market, while high DOM (over 90 days) indicates a slower buyer's market. Properties with high DOM may indicate overpricing or property issues, and sellers with high-DOM listings are often more motivated to negotiate.

A tax deduction that allows real estate investors to deduct the cost of the building (not land) over its useful life — 27.5 years for residential and 39 years for commercial properties. Depreciation reduces taxable income without requiring any actual cash expense. For example, a $275,000 building can be depreciated at $10,000 per year, reducing your tax bill even while the property appreciates in value.

A marketing strategy where investors send physical letters, postcards, or yellow letters to targeted property owners to generate motivated seller leads. Effective direct mail campaigns target specific lists: absentee owners, pre-foreclosures, tax delinquent properties, probate, and high-equity homeowners. Response rates typically range from 0.5-3% with an average cost of $0.50-$2.00 per piece.

A property in poor condition or facing financial distress such as foreclosure, tax delinquency, or code violations. Distressed properties are typically sold below market value because the owner cannot afford repairs or mortgage payments. These properties represent the primary deal source for wholesalers, flippers, and BRRRR investors. Signs of distress include overgrown lawns, boarded windows, vacant appearance, and accumulated mail.

A deal-finding strategy where investors drive through target neighborhoods looking for distressed or vacant properties. When a potential deal is spotted, the investor records the address, then uses skip tracing to find the owner's contact information and reach out with an offer. Driving for dollars is one of the cheapest and most effective lead generation methods — it costs only gas money and can be enhanced with apps like DealMachine.

A mortgage insured by the Federal Housing Administration that requires only 3.5% down payment for owner-occupied properties. FHA loans are popular for house hacking — buying a 2-4 unit property, living in one unit, and renting the others. FHA loans have lower credit requirements (580+ credit score) and lower down payments than conventional investment property loans, making them an accessible entry point for new investors.

The process of attracting and identifying potential motivated sellers or investment property leads. Lead generation methods include direct mail, cold calling, text messaging, PPC advertising, driving for dollars, bandit signs, networking, and AI-powered property search tools like AIWholesail. The quality and volume of leads directly determines deal flow and revenue for real estate investors.

The period between when a homeowner receives a notice of default from their lender and the actual foreclosure auction. During pre-foreclosure, the homeowner can still sell the property to avoid foreclosure — often at a discount because of urgency. Pre-foreclosure lists are available through county records and data services. These homeowners are among the most motivated sellers because they face losing their property entirely.

A financing arrangement where the property seller acts as the lender instead of a bank. The buyer makes monthly payments directly to the seller according to agreed-upon terms (interest rate, duration, down payment). Seller financing eliminates bank qualification requirements, appraisals, and lengthy approval processes. Common terms: 5-10% down, 6-9% interest, 5-30 year amortization with a 3-7 year balloon payment.

A company that verifies legal ownership of a property, conducts title searches, issues title insurance, and facilitates the closing process. Title companies ensure the seller has clear title (no outstanding liens, judgments, or ownership disputes) before the buyer takes ownership. In wholesale transactions, the title company holds the earnest money, processes the assignment, and distributes funds at closing.

An examination of public records to verify the legal ownership of a property and identify any liens, encumbrances, easements, or claims against it. A clear title means there are no outstanding issues that could prevent or complicate the sale. Title searches reveal mortgage liens, tax liens, mechanic's liens, judgments, and ownership history. Never close on a property without a clear title search.

Short-term financing (typically 1-3 days) used by wholesalers for double closings. A transactional funder provides the cash needed to purchase the property from the seller (A-to-B closing) and is repaid when the wholesaler immediately resells to the end buyer (B-to-C closing). Transactional funding fees are typically 1-2% of the purchase price and eliminate the need for the wholesaler to use their own capital.

The percentage of time a rental property sits empty without a paying tenant. Vacancy rate is a critical factor in cash flow analysis — most investors budget 5-10% for vacancy when projecting income. Formula: Vacancy Rate = (Vacant Days / Total Days) × 100. Market-wide vacancy rates indicate rental demand — rates below 5% suggest strong demand, while rates above 10% indicate oversupply.

A real estate investment strategy where an investor (wholesaler) puts a property under contract at a discounted price and then assigns that contract to an end buyer for a fee, without ever purchasing or owning the property. Wholesaling requires minimal capital, no renovation experience, and no property ownership. It is the most common entry point for new real estate investors and generates income through assignment fees.

A form of seller financing where the seller carries a new mortgage that wraps around their existing mortgage. The buyer makes one payment to the seller, who then continues making payments on the original loan. The seller profits from the interest rate spread between the two loans. Wraps are used in creative financing deals but carry risk if the buyer defaults or the underlying lender discovers the arrangement.

Local government regulations that dictate how a property can be used — residential, commercial, industrial, mixed-use, or agricultural. Zoning affects what you can build, how many units a property can have, and whether you can operate a business from the location. Investors should always verify zoning before purchasing — rezoning can dramatically increase a property's value, while zoning restrictions can limit your investment strategy.

The annualized rate of return that makes the net present value of all cash flows from an investment equal to zero. IRR accounts for the time value of money and is considered one of the most comprehensive metrics for evaluating real estate investments. Unlike cap rate or cash-on-cash return, IRR factors in the timing and magnitude of every cash inflow and outflow over the entire holding period, including the eventual sale. Most institutional investors target an IRR of 15-25% for value-add deals and 8-12% for stabilized core assets.

The percentage of a borrower's gross monthly income that goes toward paying monthly debt obligations. DTI = Total Monthly Debt Payments / Gross Monthly Income x 100. Lenders use DTI to assess a borrower's ability to manage monthly payments and repay debts. For conventional mortgages, lenders typically require a DTI below 43-45%. For investment property loans, DTI requirements can be stricter at 36-41%. A lower DTI signals less risk to lenders and can qualify you for better interest rates and loan terms.

A metric that compares the median home price in a market to the median annual rent, used to evaluate whether it is more economical to buy or rent — and whether a market favors rental investors. Formula: Price-to-Rent Ratio = Median Home Price / Median Annual Rent. A ratio below 15 generally indicates a strong buy market with favorable rental yields. A ratio between 15 and 20 is neutral, and above 20 suggests renting may be more attractive. Investors use this ratio to screen entire metro areas before drilling into individual deals.

The percentage of gross operating income required to cover all operating expenses and debt service. Formula: BER = (Operating Expenses + Debt Service) / Gross Operating Income x 100. A BER below 85% is generally considered acceptable by lenders, indicating the property has a cushion to absorb vacancies or unexpected expenses. A lower BER means the property can sustain higher vacancy rates before going cash-flow negative. This metric is particularly important for multifamily underwriting and commercial loan applications.

The ratio of a property's total operating expenses to its gross operating income, expressed as a percentage. Formula: OER = Operating Expenses / Gross Operating Income x 100. Operating expenses include property taxes, insurance, maintenance, management fees, and utilities but exclude debt service and capital expenditures. A typical OER for residential rental properties ranges from 35-45%, while commercial properties may run 25-40%. A lower OER indicates more efficient property operations and higher profit margins.

The total cash distributions received from an investment divided by the total equity invested. Formula: Equity Multiple = Total Distributions / Total Equity Invested. An equity multiple of 2.0x means the investor doubled their money over the life of the investment. Unlike IRR, the equity multiple does not account for the time it takes to achieve returns — a 2.0x multiple over 3 years is far better than 2.0x over 10 years. Syndication sponsors typically target equity multiples of 1.5-2.5x over a 3-7 year hold period.

Also known as the Multiple on Invested Capital (MOIC), the cash multiple measures total cash returned to an investor relative to their initial cash investment. Cash Multiple = Total Cash Received / Total Cash Invested. A cash multiple of 1.0x means you got your money back with no profit. A multiple of 2.5x means you received 2.5 times your original investment. Cash multiples are commonly used in private equity real estate and syndication deals to communicate overall return magnitude independent of time horizon or leverage.

A variation of IRR that addresses the reinvestment rate assumption. Standard IRR assumes interim cash flows are reinvested at the IRR itself, which can be unrealistically high. MIRR instead assumes positive cash flows are reinvested at the investor's cost of capital or a specified reinvestment rate, and financing costs are factored at a separate finance rate. MIRR provides a more conservative and often more realistic picture of investment performance, especially for projects with irregular cash flow patterns or very high standard IRR results.

The interest rate used to determine the present value of future cash flows in a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis. The discount rate reflects the investor's required rate of return, accounting for the risk of the investment, inflation, and the opportunity cost of capital. Higher-risk investments require higher discount rates. In real estate, typical discount rates range from 7-12% for stabilized properties and 12-20% for value-add or development projects. Selecting the right discount rate is critical because small changes dramatically affect the calculated property value.

The foundational financial concept that a dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future because of its potential earning capacity. TVM underpins virtually every real estate investment analysis — it is the basis for calculating present value, future value, net present value, and internal rate of return. Understanding TVM is essential for comparing investment opportunities with different cash flow timings, evaluating whether to pay cash versus finance, and determining the true cost of holding a property versus selling. Inflation, opportunity cost, and risk all influence TVM calculations.

A residential building divided into two separate living units, each with its own entrance, kitchen, bathroom, and living space. Duplexes are popular among house hackers because the owner can live in one unit and rent the other to offset mortgage costs. They qualify for owner-occupied financing (FHA, VA, conventional) when the owner lives on-site, requiring as little as 3.5% down. Duplexes offer a lower barrier to entry into multifamily investing compared to larger properties while still providing rental income and building real estate investing experience.

A residential building containing three separate living units under one roof or in a connected structure. Like duplexes, triplexes qualify for owner-occupied financing when the owner lives in one of the units, making them an excellent vehicle for house hacking. With two rental units covering the mortgage while you live in the third, triplexes often produce stronger cash flow than duplexes. They are classified as residential (1-4 units) rather than commercial, so they avoid the more complex commercial lending requirements.

A residential building with four separate living units, representing the maximum number of units that still qualifies for residential financing. Fourplexes are the sweet spot for many investors — they produce the most rental income of any property that can be purchased with an FHA loan (3.5% down, owner-occupied). Three rental units can often cover the entire mortgage, taxes, and insurance, letting the owner live rent-free. Fourplexes bridge the gap between small residential investing and larger commercial multifamily, offering meaningful cash flow without commercial loan requirements.

A property that combines two or more types of use — typically residential and commercial — within a single building or development. A common example is a building with retail or office space on the ground floor and apartments above. Mixed-use properties can generate diversified income streams and often command premium rents due to the convenience factor. Zoning must specifically allow mixed-use designation. Financing can be more complex since lenders may classify the property as commercial regardless of unit count, requiring larger down payments and different underwriting criteria.

A detached single-family home purchased and held as a rental investment rather than an owner-occupied residence. SFRs are the most common entry point for individual real estate investors because they are widely available, easy to finance, and simple to manage. Tenants typically handle yard care and minor maintenance, reducing management burden. SFRs tend to appreciate more consistently than multifamily properties in many markets and attract longer-term tenants, reducing turnover costs. The trade-off is lower cash flow per unit compared to multifamily properties.

A legal document that transfers whatever ownership interest the grantor has in a property to the grantee, without making any warranties or guarantees about the quality of that title. Unlike a warranty deed, a quit claim deed offers no protection against liens, encumbrances, or competing claims. Quit claim deeds are commonly used between family members, in divorce settlements, to clear title defects, or to transfer property into an LLC. They should never be used in arms-length purchase transactions because the buyer has no recourse if title issues arise.

A legal document that transfers property ownership while guaranteeing that the seller holds clear title and has the right to sell. A general warranty deed provides the strongest buyer protection — the seller warrants against all title defects, even those that occurred before they owned the property. A special warranty deed only covers the period of the seller's ownership. Warranty deeds are the standard instrument in most real estate purchase transactions and are required by most lenders and title insurance companies for financed purchases.

A legal document used in many states instead of a traditional mortgage to secure a real estate loan. A deed of trust involves three parties: the borrower (trustor), the lender (beneficiary), and a neutral third party (trustee) who holds the legal title until the loan is paid off. If the borrower defaults, the trustee can initiate a non-judicial foreclosure, which is typically faster and less expensive than the judicial foreclosure process required with traditional mortgages. About half of U.S. states use deeds of trust as the primary security instrument for real estate loans.

A legal claim or encumbrance placed on a property as security for a debt or obligation. Liens must be resolved before a property can be sold with clear title. Common types include mortgage liens (from the lender), tax liens (from unpaid property taxes), mechanic's liens (from unpaid contractors), and judgment liens (from court rulings). Lien priority determines the order in which creditors are paid — typically property tax liens take first priority, followed by the first mortgage, then subsequent liens in chronological order. A title search reveals all recorded liens.

Any claim, lien, charge, or liability attached to a property that may diminish its value or restrict its use. Encumbrances include mortgages, liens, easements, deed restrictions, zoning ordinances, and HOA rules. Not all encumbrances prevent a sale, but they must be disclosed and may affect the property's marketability and value. During due diligence, investors should identify all encumbrances through a thorough title search and determine whether they are acceptable or need to be cleared before closing. Some encumbrances, like utility easements, are common and generally harmless.

A legal right granted to a person or entity to use another person's land for a specific purpose without owning it. Common easements include utility easements (allowing power, water, or sewer lines to cross the property), access easements (providing a neighbor access through your land to reach their property), and drainage easements. Easements run with the land, meaning they transfer with property ownership. Investors should identify all easements during due diligence because they can restrict building locations, reduce usable land area, and affect property value.

A contractual right that gives a specified party the opportunity to match any offer before the property owner can sell to someone else. If the owner receives a third-party offer they want to accept, they must first present it to the ROFR holder, who can either match the terms and purchase the property or decline and allow the sale to proceed. ROFRs are common in tenant leases, partnership agreements, and HOA bylaws. For investors, having a ROFR on a desirable property secures future acquisition opportunities, while being subject to a ROFR can complicate and delay a sale.

A contract that gives the buyer the exclusive right — but not the obligation — to purchase a property at a specified price within a defined time period. The buyer pays an option fee (option consideration) for this right, which may or may not be credited toward the purchase price. Options are powerful tools for investors because they lock in a purchase price while allowing time for due diligence, securing financing, obtaining permits, or waiting for market conditions to improve. If the buyer does not exercise the option, they forfeit the option fee but have no further obligation.

A contract that combines a standard lease agreement with an option to purchase the property at a predetermined price during or at the end of the lease term. The tenant pays an upfront option fee and often a monthly rent premium, a portion of which may be credited toward the purchase price. Lease options give the tenant time to build credit, save for a down payment, or test the property before committing to buy. For investors, lease options generate income from the option fee, higher monthly rent, and potential sale. If the tenant does not exercise the option, the investor keeps all payments.

A provision in a mortgage contract that allows the lender to demand full repayment of the loan if the property is sold or transferred without the lender's consent. The due-on-sale clause is the primary risk in subject-to and wraparound mortgage transactions because the lender could theoretically call the loan due when ownership changes hands. In practice, lenders rarely enforce this clause as long as payments are current, but the risk exists. The Garn-St. Germain Act of 1982 provides specific exemptions where the clause cannot be enforced, such as transfers to a living trust or between spouses.

A market condition where the supply of homes for sale exceeds buyer demand, giving buyers more negotiating power. Indicators include rising inventory levels, longer days on market, frequent price reductions, and months of supply above 6. In a buyer's market, investors can negotiate lower prices, request seller concessions (closing cost credits, repairs, extended due diligence), and take more time with decisions. Buyer's markets often present the best acquisition opportunities for wholesalers and buy-and-hold investors who can purchase below market value.

A market condition where buyer demand exceeds the available supply of homes, giving sellers the upper hand in negotiations. Indicators include low inventory, multiple offers, bidding wars, homes selling above asking price, and months of supply below 3. In a seller's market, investors face more competition and tighter margins on deals. Wholesalers may find fewer motivated sellers willing to discount, but they can sell contracts faster to eager end buyers. Flippers face higher acquisition costs but may benefit from rapidly appreciating ARVs. Strategy adjustments are critical in seller's markets.

The total number of homes currently available for sale in a given market at a specific point in time. Housing inventory is a key supply-side indicator that drives market dynamics — low inventory creates competition and pushes prices up, while high inventory gives buyers leverage and can depress prices. Inventory is typically measured as active listings on the MLS and is often expressed as months of supply for easier interpretation. Investors track inventory trends to time acquisitions, predict price movements, and identify markets transitioning between buyer's and seller's conditions.

A metric that indicates how long it would take to sell all currently listed homes at the current pace of sales, assuming no new listings are added. Formula: Months of Supply = Active Listings / Monthly Sales Rate. A balanced market typically has 4-6 months of supply. Below 4 months indicates a seller's market with upward price pressure. Above 6 months indicates a buyer's market with potential price declines. Months of supply is one of the most reliable leading indicators for predicting near-term home price direction in a local market.

The recurring pattern of expansion, peak, contraction, and trough that real estate markets experience over time. A full cycle typically lasts 7-18 years. During expansion, prices rise, construction increases, and vacancies drop. At the peak, prices plateau and speculation intensifies. During contraction, prices decline, inventory rises, and distressed properties increase. At the trough, prices bottom out and savvy investors acquire properties at deep discounts. Understanding where a market sits in its cycle is essential for timing investments, selecting strategies, and managing risk.

Upfront fees charged by a lender at closing, calculated as a percentage of the loan amount. One point equals 1% of the loan. There are two types: origination points (lender's fee for processing the loan) and discount points (prepaid interest to buy down the interest rate). For example, 2 points on a $200,000 loan equals $4,000 due at closing. Hard money lenders typically charge 2-4 origination points, while conventional lenders may charge 0-1 points. Points must be factored into the total cost of borrowing when comparing loan options and analyzing deal profitability.

The process of paying off a loan through regular installment payments over a set period, where each payment covers both principal and interest. In the early years of an amortized loan, most of each payment goes toward interest, with the principal portion increasing over time. A standard residential mortgage uses a 30-year amortization schedule. Shorter amortization periods (15 or 20 years) result in higher monthly payments but significantly less total interest paid and faster equity buildup. Investors analyze amortization schedules to understand equity accumulation and true borrowing costs over the hold period.

A large lump-sum payment due at the end of a loan term that has not been fully amortized. Balloon loans typically feature lower monthly payments during the loan term because the full principal is not paid down — the remaining balance comes due all at once. For example, a loan with a 30-year amortization schedule but a 5-year balloon term requires monthly payments calculated on 30 years, but the entire remaining balance is due after 5 years. Balloon payments are common in seller financing, commercial loans, and some hard money loans. Borrowers must refinance or sell before the balloon date.

A loan where the borrower pays only the interest for a specified period (typically 3-10 years) before the loan converts to fully amortizing payments or requires a balloon payment. Monthly payments are lower during the interest-only period because no principal is being paid down, which maximizes cash flow. However, no equity is built through payment, and once the IO period ends, payments increase substantially. Interest-only loans are commonly used by flippers (who plan to sell before principal payments begin) and buy-and-hold investors seeking to maximize initial cash flow.

A short-term loan (typically 6-36 months) used to bridge the gap between purchasing a new property and securing long-term financing or selling an existing property. Bridge loans provide fast capital with flexible terms but come with higher interest rates (8-14%) and origination fees (1-3 points) compared to permanent financing. They are commonly used when an investor needs to close quickly on a time-sensitive deal, when renovating a property before qualifying for conventional financing, or when transitioning between properties. Bridge loans are similar to hard money loans but often offered by banks and credit unions.

A single mortgage that covers two or more properties under one loan, allowing investors to finance multiple acquisitions without taking out separate loans for each property. Blanket mortgages include a partial release clause that lets the borrower sell individual properties from the portfolio without paying off the entire loan. This financing tool is popular among investors building rental portfolios, developers purchasing multiple lots, and anyone consolidating multiple property loans. Benefits include simplified management, potentially lower interest rates, and cross-collateralization. The main risk is that defaulting affects all properties in the blanket.

A mortgage loan that a bank or lender originates and retains in its own investment portfolio rather than selling on the secondary market to Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Because portfolio loans are not sold, the lender sets its own underwriting criteria, making them more flexible than conventional loans. Portfolio lenders can finance properties that do not meet standard guidelines — more than 10 financed properties, unusual property types, or borrowers with non-traditional income. Terms vary by lender but typically include slightly higher rates and shorter amortization periods in exchange for greater flexibility.

A loan where the lender's only remedy in case of default is to seize the collateral property — the borrower is not personally liable for any deficiency between the property's value and the loan balance. If the property sells for less than the outstanding debt, the lender absorbs the loss. Non-recourse loans are common in commercial real estate and are a key feature of many DSCR loans and syndication financing structures. They protect the investor's personal assets but typically require higher down payments (25-35%), stronger property fundamentals, and may carry higher interest rates to offset the lender's increased risk.

A real estate strategy where the investor gets the seller to sign a listing agreement authorizing the investor to sell the property on the open market, typically at full retail value, while keeping the spread between the list price and the agreed-upon price with the seller. Unlike wholesaling, novation allows properties to be listed on the MLS and marketed to retail buyers and financed buyers, dramatically expanding the buyer pool. The investor never takes title to the property — the seller sells directly to the end buyer at closing. Novation deals often produce larger profits than traditional wholesale assignments because they access the retail market.

A hybrid strategy between wholesaling and retailing where the investor purchases a property, performs minimal cosmetic improvements (cleaning, paint, landscaping), and resells it quickly on the MLS at a price below full retail but above the wholesale price. Wholetailing targets properties that need light work — not full rehabs — and sells to retail buyers or owner-occupants rather than investors. Profits typically range from $15,000 to $40,000 per deal. The strategy fills the gap between pure wholesaling (no ownership) and full fix-and-flip (extensive renovation), offering moderate returns with less risk and faster turnaround.

A wholesaling approach where the investor first identifies what their end buyers want — property type, location, price range, condition — and then goes out to find properties that match those specific criteria. This is the reverse of traditional wholesaling, where you find a deal first and then look for a buyer. Reverse wholesaling reduces the risk of being stuck with a contract you cannot assign because the buyer is already lined up. The strategy requires building strong relationships with cash buyers and deeply understanding their acquisition criteria before sourcing deals.

An investment strategy where an investor purchases undeveloped or raw land in the path of growth and holds it for future appreciation or development. The premise is that expanding metro areas, new infrastructure projects, and population growth will eventually increase demand for the land, driving up its value. Land banking requires patience — holding periods can range from 5 to 20+ years — and carries unique risks including lack of income during the hold period, property taxes, zoning changes, and environmental issues. When successful, land banking can produce extraordinary returns as rural or suburban parcels become prime development sites.

The legal process by which a lender seizes and sells a property after the borrower defaults on their mortgage payments. Foreclosure can be judicial (through the court system) or non-judicial (through a power-of-sale clause in the deed of trust), depending on state law. The foreclosure timeline ranges from a few months to over a year. Investors can acquire foreclosed properties at auction, through bank-owned (REO) listings after the auction, or by negotiating with homeowners during the pre-foreclosure period. Foreclosure purchases often come at significant discounts but may involve title issues, property damage, or occupancy challenges.

A thorough examination of a property's physical condition conducted by a licensed home inspector or qualified professional during the due diligence period. A standard inspection covers the roof, foundation, structural components, electrical system, plumbing, HVAC, insulation, windows, and appliances. The inspection report identifies existing defects, potential problems, and safety hazards. For investors, inspections are critical for validating rehab cost estimates and avoiding costly surprises after closing. Specialized inspections may include termite, mold, radon, sewer scope, and environmental assessments depending on the property.

The ongoing expenses an investor pays while owning a property before it is sold or rented. Holding costs include mortgage payments, property taxes, insurance, utilities, HOA fees, lawn maintenance, security, and any loan interest. For fix-and-flip investors, holding costs are a critical component of deal analysis because they erode profit with every additional month the project takes. Typical holding costs range from $1,500 to $5,000+ per month depending on the property value and location. Minimizing holding costs requires efficient project management, realistic timelines, and quick turnaround on renovations.

Documentation that demonstrates a buyer has sufficient capital to close on a real estate transaction. Proof of funds typically includes bank statements, investment account statements, or a letter from a lender or transactional funding source confirming available capital. Sellers and their agents request proof of funds to verify the buyer is legitimate before accepting an offer. Wholesalers often use proof-of-funds letters from hard money lenders or transactional funders even if they plan to assign the contract rather than close. Having ready proof of funds strengthens your offers and builds credibility with sellers.

The process of selling or otherwise transferring a property or contract to an end buyer. In wholesaling, disposition refers to finding a cash buyer and assigning the contract or executing a double closing. In fix-and-flip, disposition is listing and selling the renovated property. Effective disposition strategies include maintaining a robust buyers list, marketing to investor networks, listing on the MLS, and leveraging social media and real estate platforms. Speed of disposition directly impacts profitability — the faster you sell, the lower your holding costs and the sooner you can recycle capital into the next deal.

The process of identifying, negotiating, and purchasing an investment property. Successful acquisition requires deal sourcing (finding opportunities through marketing, networking, or MLS), underwriting (analyzing the numbers to determine profitability), negotiation (structuring favorable terms with the seller), and closing (completing the legal transfer). The acquisition phase determines the profit potential of every deal — buying right is the most important skill in real estate investing. The saying 'you make your money when you buy, not when you sell' underscores that disciplined acquisition is the foundation of investment success.

In real estate investing, capitalization refers to the process of converting a property's net operating income into an estimate of its value using the capitalization rate. The formula — Value = NOI / Cap Rate — is the foundational valuation method for income-producing properties. Capitalization is also used more broadly to describe funding or financing an investment. The concept is central to understanding how commercial and multifamily properties are valued: since these properties are priced based on income rather than comparable sales, improving NOI through higher rents or lower expenses directly increases the property's capitalized value.

A partnership structure where a sponsor (general partner) pools capital from multiple passive investors (limited partners) to acquire and operate a real estate asset that none could afford individually. The sponsor handles all active management — sourcing deals, arranging financing, overseeing renovations, and managing the property — while investors provide capital and receive a share of cash flow and profits. Syndications are typically structured as LLCs and may be regulated by the SEC as securities offerings. Common return structures include a preferred return (6-10%) to investors before the sponsor takes a promote (profit split), with target equity multiples of 1.5-2.5x over a 3-7 year hold.
